Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd is a company incorporated in Singapore on 5 August 2020 with an initial paid-up capital of $100,000.

As at incorporation, AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd had three shareholders: Liew Nyok Wah holding 33% of the shares, Teo Ying Ping holding 33% of the shares, and Ho Siow Poh holding 34% of the shares.
From incorporation, Ho Siow Poh was the Managing Director of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd and its sole director on record.
In 2021, Marcus Teo Xian Jun was appointed Chief Financial Officer of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd.
Shortly after the incorporation of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd, Liew Shulin Mrs Alexander Chong joined the company and was involved in its finance and business operations.
From the inception of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd, both Teo Ying Ping and Liew Nyok Wah provided funds to the company using their personal funds.

At later stages, some funding for AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd was provided by Teo Ying Ping and Liew Nyok Wah through loans obtained from third parties to them personally, with onward transfers of funds to the company or directly as loans to the company.
Throughout the life of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd up to May 2023, both Teo Ying Ping and Liew Nyok Wah continued to contribute funds to the company.
In or around May 2023, disagreements arose among the three shareholders of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd concerning the company’s funding arrangements.
On 25 July 2023, AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd commenced legal proceedings against shareholder Liew Nyok Wah.
On 27 July 2023, the employment of Liew Shulin Mrs Alexander Chong with AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd was terminated.
The Accounting and Corporate Regulatory Authority filings for AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd recorded its share capital as $100,000 during the relevant period prior to the dispute.
Financial statements of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd recorded its share capital as $100,000 during the relevant period prior to the dispute.

Internal accounting records of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd recorded funds provided by both Teo Ying Ping and Liew Nyok Wah under headings such as 'Loan from Director' and in loan schedules with fields including 'Date of Loan', 'Expiry Date of Loan', and 'Loan interest/month'.
In November 2020, both Ho Siow Poh and Teo Ying Ping noticed that contributions from shareholders were labelled as 'directors’ loans' in the accounting records of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd.

Liew Shulin regularly prepared a 'loan sheet' for AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd, listing monies received with loan-related headers, and shared it in a WhatsApp chat group named 'AP Accounting Department' that included, among others, Teo Ying Ping and Marcus Teo.
After August 2023, following the departure of Liew Shulin from AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd, Ho Siow Poh took over the company’s financial documents and accounting records.
On 9 January 2023, a Creditors Confirmation Request was signed on behalf of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd confirming that the company owed $1.03 million to Liew Nyok Wah as at 31 July 2022.

As at the time shortly before the dispute, the most updated accounts of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd, intended for adoption at its Annual General Meeting, reflected amounts owed to both Liew Nyok Wah and Teo Ying Ping as loans.

On 7 April 2021, AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd received a contribution of $100,000 associated with a cheque dated 1 April 2021 from Teo Ying Ping, described in a WhatsApp message to Liew Shulin as 'for short term for 2 months repayment and interest rate is 1.5% per month'.

On or about 7 April 2021, in a WhatsApp message to Liew Shulin, Teo Ying Ping stated that he had passed a cheque for $100,000 dated 1 April 2021 to Ho Siow Poh and described it as a short-term arrangement for two months with interest at 1.5% per month.

AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd obtained a loan of $300,000 from Junk Yard Dog Ventures Pte Ltd, a company associated with the brother of shareholder Teo Ying Ping, and this amount was recorded in the company’s accounting records as a loan from Junk Yard Dog.
Of the $300,000 loan from Junk Yard Dog Ventures Pte Ltd to AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd, $200,000 was repaid between 1 June 2022 and 12 July 2022.
On 14 January 2021, AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd made a payment of $160,000 to or for the benefit of shareholder Teo Ying Ping.
On 20 December 2022, AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd made a payment of $15,000 to or for the benefit of shareholder Teo Ying Ping.

On 8 October 2021, AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd received approximately $260,000 in funds associated with a hire-purchase loan of $260,000 taken by shareholder Teo Ying Ping over his car, under which a total of $311,740 was to be repaid over 60 monthly instalments of $5,196.

Following the 8 October 2021 hire-purchase arrangement on the car of Teo Ying Ping, AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd began paying monthly instalments of $5,196 to the finance company on behalf of Teo Ying Ping, covering both principal and interest, until the company ran out of funds a few months later.

From the $260,000 hire-purchase loan proceeds associated with the car of Teo Ying Ping, approximately $60,000 was used for the renewal of the car’s Certificate of Entitlement and approximately $200,000 was made available to AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd.

As of 8 October 2021, based on internal records, net contributions to AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd (excluding initial paid-up capital) were approximately $2,124,407 associated with Teo Ying Ping and approximately $1.8 million associated with or arranged by Liew Nyok Wah.
On 8 January 2021, AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd entered into a loan agreement with Mengkim Holdings Pte Ltd for a principal sum of $500,000 at an interest rate of 10% per annum.

The repayment date of the $500,000 loan from Mengkim Holdings Pte Ltd to AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd was extended first to 8 April 2022 and then to 8 July 2022, and the loan was repaid in instalments with the final repayment on 28 April 2023.

Between 21 November 2021 and 21 January 2022, AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd repaid a total of $200,000 to or in respect of a loan from an individual named Lee Sze Peng, a friend of Managing Director Ho Siow Poh.
Between 19 November 2021 and May 2022, AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd repaid a total of $200,000 to WQ Leasing Pte Ltd.

On 31 May 2022, WhatsApp messages between Marcus Teo and Liew Shulin discussed structuring the repayment of the $200,000 balance of the Junk Yard Dog Ventures Pte Ltd loan in two parts to accommodate pending car sales.
In 2021 and 2022, AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd sold a number of cars from its fleet, raising slightly over $1 million after paying off loans secured on those cars.
By 2021, AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd had moved its workshop operations from premises in Tampines to premises in Loyang.

After moving workshop operations to Loyang in early 2021, AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd continued to retain an office unit on the second storey and a room on the first storey at the Tampines premises.
The Tampines premises remained the registered office address of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd until 24 August 2023.

AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd paid a monthly retainer fee to Jackspeed Automobile (S) Pte Ltd for office space and related arrangements at the Tampines premises from at least March 2021 to May 2023, with a reduction in the fee after the move of workshop operations to Loyang.
AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd made interest payments at rates between 10% and 18% per annum on certain third-party loans, including loans associated with funding arranged by shareholders.
AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd paid interest at rates above 8% per annum, and up to 18% per annum, on some funds associated with contributions or loans linked to shareholder Teo Ying Ping.

On 2 May 2023, a meeting took place between shareholders Ho Siow Poh, Teo Ying Ping, and Liew Nyok Wah, which was recorded and later transcribed, during which they discussed funding of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd and potential buy-out figures in the range of $500,000 to $700,000.

By May 2023, the relationship among the three shareholders of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd had become strained, particularly in relation to the company’s funding, leading to discussions about restructuring and potential buy-outs.

In or around August or September 2021, a restructuring plan for AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd was proposed by Chief Financial Officer Marcus Teo, which included the sale of a significant portion of the company’s car fleet to reduce liabilities.
By late 2021, AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd had already sold more than 20 cars from its fleet as part of its efforts to improve its financial position.

AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd made repayments on loans associated with or arranged by shareholder Liew Nyok Wah, including loans from individuals such as Tan Bee Hong and an entity named Ban Leong, starting around 1 May 2022.
In the financial year ending 31 July 2022, AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd repaid approximately $220,000 of the combined $400,000 principal associated with loans from Tan Bee Hong and Ban Leong.
Prior to the incorporation of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd, shareholders Ho Siow Poh, Teo Ying Ping, and Liew Nyok Wah had been long-term friends.
Junk Yard Dog Ventures Pte Ltd, which provided a $300,000 loan to AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd, is associated with the brother of shareholder Teo Ying Ping.
Jackspeed Automobile (S) Pte Ltd, which received retainer fees from AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd for office space and related services, is an entity connected to shareholder Liew Nyok Wah.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether funds provided to AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd by shareholders and third parties prior to the dispute should be characterised as shareholder loans or capital contributions, and whether repayments and interest payments on those funds, as well as asset sales and retainer payments, affected the company’s financial position in a manner giving rise to liability between the company and shareholder Liew Nyok Wah.
Party_Details.Rule: In company law, the legal characterisation of funds provided to a company as debt or equity generally depends on the terms of the arrangement, including documentation, accounting treatment, repayment expectations, and any agreed interest, and the company’s obligations to repay such funds and pay interest are determined by those arrangements and applicable statutory and common law principles governing loans, share capital, and directors’ and shareholders’ conduct.
Party_Details.Application: The pre-dispute records of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd, including Accounting and Corporate Regulatory Authority filings, financial statements, internal accounting entries, loan schedules, WhatsApp communications, and creditor confirmations, consistently treated funds from shareholders and related entities as loans with specified interest rates and repayment terms. The company entered into formal loan arrangements with third parties such as Mengkim Holdings Pte Ltd and Junk Yard Dog Ventures Pte Ltd, and made repayments and interest payments on those loans and on shareholder-linked funding. The company also sold vehicles and used sale proceeds to reduce various liabilities, while continuing to pay a retainer to Jackspeed Automobile (S) Pte Ltd for office and storage space at the Tampines premises, which remained its registered office until August 2023. These observable pre-dispute events frame the financial relationship between AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd and shareholder Liew Nyok Wah and the amounts outstanding between them at the time the dispute arose.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judgment concludes that AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd’s claim against Liew Nyok Wah fails and is dismissed, primarily because the court finds that the funds provided by both Teo Ying Ping and Liew Nyok Wah were loans rather than capital injections, and that there was no enforceable agreement requiring matching capital or funding contributions beyond the initial paid-up capital. The court analyses the alleged 'Agreement' and the later 'Matching Dollar Agreement' and determines that the evidence does not support a binding obligation on Liew Nyok Wah to match Teo Ying Ping’s contributions as capital or otherwise, nor an implied restriction on the timing of withdrawals similar to the arrangement in Wee Kah Lee v Silverdale Investment Pte Ltd. The court places significant weight on contemporaneous documents: ACRA filings and financial statements consistently showing share capital at $100,000; internal accounting records and loan sheets classifying shareholder and related-party funds as 'Loan from Director' or similar; the Creditors Confirmation Request acknowledging $1.03 million owed to Liew as at 31 July 2022; and the absence of any internal documents describing the contributions as capital. The court finds that Teo’s own funding behaviour, including the $100,000 short-term loan at 1.5% per month, the $300,000 Junk Yard Dog loan, the hire-purchase arrangement on his car, and repayments of $160,000 and $15,000 to him, is consistent with loan treatment and inconsistent with capital contributions. The court also notes that Ho and Teo did not object when Liew arranged third-party loans such as the Mengkim loan, and that Teo himself arranged the Junk Yard Dog loan, indicating that the company was concerned with obtaining funding rather than enforcing any strict capital-matching obligation. On the alleged Matching Dollar Agreement, the court finds that the company’s case was not pleaded, that contributions were ad hoc and not kept in strict parity, and that there was no attempt to enforce matching when one shareholder was ahead or behind. The court rejects the proposed implied term restricting withdrawals until profitability or sufficient funding, distinguishing Wee Kah Lee on the basis that the loans there were tied to a specific development project with a defined timeframe, whereas here the loans were for general operations with no such framework. Regarding alleged breaches of fiduciary duties, the court proceeds on the assumption (without deciding) that Liew might be a de facto or shadow director but holds that, since his contributions were loans and there was no restriction on repayment, he was entitled to be repaid and his receipt of repayments cannot constitute a breach of fiduciary duty. The court finds that Ho, as Managing Director, was aware of and approved the repayments, and rejects the explanation that Ho acted under pressure from Liew, noting inconsistencies in Ho’s evidence and the absence of any pleaded or proven duress or undue influence. The court also rejects the contention that Ho acted under Liew’s directions or that he was accustomed to doing so, as this was not pleaded and not supported by evidence. The court narrows the company’s fiduciary-duty case to repayments of loans from Tan Bee Hong and Ban Leong but finds that the loans remained classified as 'Loan from Director', that any alleged reclassification to external loans was not pleaded and did not occur, and that Ho knew the funds originated from Liew and that there were no direct loan agreements with those third parties. On damages, the court notes that the company abandoned several heads of claim (interest rate difference, retainer fees, and the loan to Chris Eng) and, in any event, finds them unsustainable. For the interest-rate-difference claim, the court observes that Ho accepted Liew could set interest rates, that Teo himself received interest up to 18% per annum, and that Ho agreed to the Mengkim loan at 10% per annum, so there is no basis to treat higher rates as wrongful. For the alleged 'fire sale' of 100 cars, the court finds that sales began before the relevant third-party loans were due, that the list of 100 cars is inaccurate, that the sale was proposed by Marcus Teo as part of a restructuring plan, and that sale proceeds were used to repay various loans including those to Lee Sze Peng, WQ Leasing, and Junk Yard Dog, not specifically or exclusively loans associated with Liew. The court characterises the fire-sale allegation as contrived and bordering on dishonesty. For the 'loss of use' claim, the court finds the methodology of comparing net profits between financial years to be legally and evidentially flawed, notes that only part of the relevant principal was repaid in the claimed period, and concludes that there is no actionable loss because the company had agreed to repay those sums and the repayments were not in breach of any duty. For the retainer-fee claim, the court finds that all three shareholders agreed to retain space at the Tampines premises, that the company’s records and equipment remained there, that the Tampines address remained the registered office until August 2023, and that Ho and Teo agreed to a reduced retainer fee; thus there is no basis to hold Liew responsible for those payments. The court notes that the company abandoned the $300,000 loan-to-Chris-Eng claim at the outset and makes no findings on it. On Liew’s status, the court remarks that the company’s position is selective in treating Liew as a de facto or shadow director while describing Teo as a 'sleeping shareholder' despite his active involvement, but ultimately finds it unnecessary to decide Liew’s status because the company’s claims fail on other grounds. On the counterclaim, the court accepts that Liew’s contributions were loans and that there is no valid defence to his claim for repayment. The company does not dispute the pleaded outstanding sum of $719,000, and the court therefore enters judgment for Liew in that amount. The court awards Liew costs of $150,000 inclusive of disbursements and indicates that it will hear parties on whether Teo should be liable for the company’s costs and invites submissions on whether Ho should be made personally liable for costs, reflecting the court’s concerns about aspects of the company’s case and the conduct of its witnesses.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Liew Nyok Wah

Facts.Text: Liew Nyok Wah is a shareholder of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd holding 33% of its shares from incorporation on 5 August 2020.
Before the incorporation of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd, Liew Nyok Wah had a long-term friendship with fellow shareholders Ho Siow Poh and Teo Ying Ping.
Liew Shulin Mrs Alexander Chong, who worked in finance and business operations at AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd, is the daughter of shareholder Liew Nyok Wah.
During the relevant period, Liew Nyok Wah was not recorded as a director of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd in official filings, while Ho Siow Poh was the sole director on record.
From the inception of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd, Liew Nyok Wah provided funds to the company using his personal funds.

At later stages, Liew Nyok Wah arranged for funding to AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd by procuring loans from third parties either to himself or directly to the company, and then transferring or facilitating the transfer of funds to the company.

Internal accounting records of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd recorded funds associated with or arranged by Liew Nyok Wah under headings such as 'Loan from Director' and in loan schedules with specified interest rates and repayment dates.
As at 31 July 2022, a Creditors Confirmation Request signed on behalf of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd recorded that the company owed $1.03 million to Liew Nyok Wah.

As of 8 October 2021, internal records of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd showed that net funds associated with or arranged by Liew Nyok Wah, excluding initial paid-up capital, totalled approximately $1.8 million.

On 8 January 2021, AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd entered into a loan agreement with Mengkim Holdings Pte Ltd for $500,000 at an interest rate of 10% per annum, with the loan being associated with funding arrangements involving shareholder Liew Nyok Wah.

The $500,000 loan from Mengkim Holdings Pte Ltd to AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd, associated with arrangements involving Liew Nyok Wah, had its repayment date extended to 8 April 2022 and then to 8 July 2022, and was repaid in instalments with the final repayment on 28 April 2023.

AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd obtained loans totalling $400,000 from individuals or entities named Tan Bee Hong and Ban Leong, which were associated with funding arrangements involving shareholder Liew Nyok Wah, and began repaying these loans around 1 May 2022.
In the financial year ending 31 July 2022, AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd repaid approximately $220,000 of the combined $400,000 principal associated with the loans from Tan Bee Hong and Ban Leong.

On 2 May 2023, during a recorded meeting with shareholders Ho Siow Poh and Teo Ying Ping, Liew Nyok Wah discussed funding of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd and potential buy-out figures in the range of $500,000 to $700,000.

In the 2 May 2023 recorded meeting, there was discussion among the shareholders about matching contributions and the overall level of funding each shareholder had provided or arranged for AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd.

By May 2023, disagreements between Liew Nyok Wah and the other shareholders over funding arrangements for AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd had escalated, contributing to the commencement of legal proceedings by the company on 25 July 2023.
After the falling out among shareholders in or around May 2023, Liew Nyok Wah ceased to be involved in the management of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd.

Jackspeed Automobile (S) Pte Ltd, which received retainer fees from AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd for office space and related services at the Tampines premises, is an entity connected to shareholder Liew Nyok Wah.

Between March 2021 and May 2023, AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd paid monthly retainer fees to Jackspeed Automobile (S) Pte Ltd for office space and related arrangements, with the fee being reduced after the move of workshop operations to Loyang.

All three shareholders, including Liew Nyok Wah, agreed that AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd would retain some space at the Tampines premises after moving workshop operations to Loyang, and that the company would continue paying a (reduced) retainer fee to Jackspeed Automobile (S) Pte Ltd.
On 25 July 2023, AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd commenced an action against shareholder Liew Nyok Wah in the General Division of the High Court of Singapore.

In response to the company’s claim, Liew Nyok Wah filed a counterclaim seeking repayment of loans he had provided or arranged for AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd, in the amount of $719,000 outstanding at the time of the dispute.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, prior to the dispute, the funds provided or arranged by shareholder Liew Nyok Wah for AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd were loans repayable by the company, and whether the company’s repayments and interest payments on those funds and related third-party loans resulted in an outstanding amount of $719,000 owed by the company to him.
Party_Details.Rule: Where a shareholder advances or arranges funds to a company under terms that are treated in documentation and accounting records as loans, with specified interest and repayment obligations, the company is generally obliged to repay the outstanding loan principal and agreed interest in accordance with those terms, subject to any contrary binding agreement or legal restriction.
Party_Details.Application: The contemporaneous records of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd, including internal accounting entries labelling funds associated with or arranged by Liew Nyok Wah as 'Loan from Director', loan schedules specifying interest rates and repayment dates, and a Creditors Confirmation Request acknowledging $1.03 million owed to him as at 31 July 2022, indicate that his funding was treated as debt. The company entered into a $500,000 loan with Mengkim Holdings Pte Ltd and other third-party loans associated with his arrangements, and made repayments and interest payments on those obligations. As of 8 October 2021, internal computations showed approximately $1.8 million in net contributions associated with or arranged by him, and by the time of the dispute, the company did not contest that $719,000 remained outstanding on loans linked to him. There is no contemporaneous pre-dispute documentation establishing a binding agreement that would convert these amounts into non-repayable capital or otherwise extinguish the company’s repayment obligation.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court holds that the company’s claim against Liew Nyok Wah fails and that his counterclaim succeeds. On the characterisation of his funding, the court finds that the evidence overwhelmingly supports the conclusion that his contributions were loans rather than capital. This conclusion is based on the consistent treatment of his funds in the company’s financial statements and internal records as 'Loan from Director', the Creditors Confirmation Request confirming $1.03 million owed to him as at 31 July 2022, and the absence of any internal or external documentation describing his contributions as share capital beyond the initial $100,000 paid-up capital. The court notes that the company’s own updated accounts, intended for adoption at its Annual General Meeting, still reflect amounts owed to him as loans, and that no satisfactory explanation was given for the failure to amend these records even after the relationship between the shareholders deteriorated and after he ceased involvement in management. The court rejects the company’s attempt to rely on an alleged Agreement or Matching Dollar Agreement to recharacterise his funding as capital or to impose restrictions on repayment. It finds that the alleged Agreement was not established on the evidence and that the Matching Dollar Agreement was not pleaded and is inconsistent with the parties’ conduct, including the ad hoc nature of contributions, the absence of enforcement when contributions were not matched, and the company’s acceptance of third-party loans arranged by both Teo and Liew. The court also rejects the proposed implied term restricting withdrawals until profitability or sufficient funding, distinguishing Wee Kah Lee on the basis that the loans in that case were tied to a specific development project with a defined timeframe, whereas here the loans were for general operations. On the alleged breach of fiduciary duties, the court assumes for argument’s sake that Liew might be a de facto or shadow director but concludes that, since his contributions were loans and there was no restriction on repayment, he was entitled to be repaid and his receipt of repayments cannot constitute a breach of fiduciary duty. The court emphasises that Ho, as Managing Director, authorised the repayments and that the company did not plead or prove any legal basis such as duress or undue influence that would vitiate Ho’s decisions. The court finds the company’s explanations about pressure from Liew to be unconvincing and internally inconsistent, and notes that key allegations about pressure or direction were not put to Liew in cross-examination. The court further finds that the company’s damages claims, including those based on interest-rate differences, alleged fire-sale losses, loss of use of funds, retainer fees, and the loan to Chris Eng, are either abandoned or unsupported by evidence and legal principle. In particular, the court finds that the fire-sale allegation is contrived, that the sale of the fleet was part of a restructuring plan proposed by Marcus Teo, and that sale proceeds were used to repay various loans, not specifically or exclusively loans associated with Liew. The court concludes that there is no valid defence to Liew’s counterclaim for repayment of his loans. The company does not dispute the pleaded outstanding sum of $719,000, and the court therefore enters judgment for Liew in that amount. The court also awards him costs of $150,000 inclusive of disbursements and indicates that it will hear further submissions on whether other individuals, including Teo and Ho, should bear costs personally, reflecting the court’s concerns about aspects of the company’s case and the conduct of its witnesses.
